notes.section_title: 10.1 Introduction: Positions of a Line and a Circle

topics.topic_name: Three Possible Positions of a Line with Respect to a Circle

content.text: When a line and a circle are given in a plane, there are exactly three possible relationships between them based on the number of common points they share.

content.title: The Three Cases
items: Non-intersecting line: The line PQ and the circle have no common point. The line does not touch or cross the circle.
Secant: The line PQ intersects the circle at two distinct points (A and B). A secant is a line that cuts through the circle.
Tangent: The line PQ touches the circle at exactly one point (A). This line is called a tangent to the circle.

content.text: These are the only three possible positions. No other configuration is possible between a line and a circle. Understanding these distinctions is fundamental to the study of tangents and their properties.

content.title: Real-World Application
content.text: A pulley fitted over a well, as seen in traditional water-drawing systems, demonstrates the concept of a tangent. The rope on both sides of the pulley acts as tangent lines to the circular pulley, touching it at exactly one point.
A tangent to a circle is a line that intersects the circle at exactly one point. At that unique point of intersection, the tangent is said to touch the circle.

content.title: Activity 1: Understanding Tangent as a Limiting Case of Secant
procedure: Take a circular wire and attach a straight wire AB at a point P on the circular wire.
Rotate the wire AB about point P in a plane.
Observe the positions where AB intersects the circle at P and another point (Q₁, Q₂, Q₃, etc.).
Continue rotating until AB intersects the circle only at point P.
content.observation: As the wire AB rotates towards the position A'B', the second point of intersection gradually moves closer to P. Eventually, it coincides with P, and the line becomes a tangent.
content.conclusion: A tangent to a circle is a special case of a secant where the two endpoints of the corresponding chord coincide. There is only one tangent at any given point on a circle.

content.title: Activity 2: Tangents Parallel to a Secant
procedure: Draw a circle and a secant PQ of the circle on a paper.
Draw multiple lines parallel to the secant on both sides of it.
Observe the lengths of the chords cut by these parallel lines.
content.observation: As the parallel lines move away from the original secant, the chord length gradually decreases. Eventually, the chord length becomes zero, indicating a tangent position.
content.conclusion: For any given secant, there are exactly two tangents parallel to it—one on each side. This demonstrates that tangents are limiting positions of secants.

content.text: The common point where a tangent touches the circle is called the point of contact. The tangent is said to touch the circle at this specific point.

content.title: Key Property
content.text: At any point on a circle, there exists exactly one tangent line. This tangent is unique and distinct from all other possible lines through that point.

topics.topic_name: Theorem 10.1: Perpendicularity of Tangent and Radius

content.title: Theorem Statement
content.text: The tangent at any point of a circle is perpendicular to the radius through the point of contact.
This is one of the most fundamental theorems in circle geometry. It establishes the geometric relationship between the tangent line and the radius at the point where they meet.

Proof: Consider a circle with centre O and a tangent XY at point P. We need to prove that OP ⊥ XY. Take any point Q on the tangent XY (other than P) and join OQ. Since Q lies on the tangent and not on the circle, Q must be outside the circle. Therefore, OQ > OP (where OP is the radius). This inequality holds for every point on line XY except point P. Consequently, OP is the shortest distance from O to any point on XY. A line is perpendicular to another line at the point where the perpendicular distance is shortest. Therefore, OP is perpendicular to XY.
This theorem has profound implications: the radius to the point of contact is always perpendicular to the tangent at that point, forming a 90° angle.

content.title: Practical Observation
content.text: When a wheel rolls on the ground, the radius through the point of contact with the ground is perpendicular to the ground (which acts as a tangent to the wheel). This is why wheels can roll smoothly—the tangent and radius are always at right angles.

content.title: Important Consequences of Theorem 10.1
items: At any point on a circle, there is one and only one tangent line.
The tangent at any point is perpendicular to the radius at that point, creating a 90° angle.
The line containing the radius through the point of contact is sometimes called the 'normal' to the circle at that point.

notes.section_title: 10.3 Number of Tangents from a Point

topics.topic_name: Tangents from Points Inside, On, and Outside the Circle

content.title: Activity 3: Exploring Tangents from Different Point Locations
procedure: Case 1: Take a point P inside the circle. Try to draw lines through P.
Case 2: Take a point P on the circle. Draw lines through P.
Case 3: Take a point P outside the circle. Draw lines through P.
content.observation: Case 1: All lines through an interior point intersect the circle at two points (secants). No tangent exists. Case 2: There is exactly one line through a point on the circle that touches it at only that point (tangent). Case 3: There are exactly two lines through an exterior point that touch the circle at exactly one point each (tangents).
content.conclusion: The number of tangents depends on the position of the point relative to the circle. The three cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive.

content.text: If a point P lies inside a circle, there is no tangent to the circle passing through this point.

Reason: Any line passing through an interior point P will intersect the circle at two distinct points (creating a secant), not at just one point. Therefore, no tangent can exist through an interior point.
If a point P lies on a circle, there is one and only one tangent to the circle passing through this point.

This is the tangent at the point P itself, as established in the previous section. Any other line through P will either intersect the circle at another point (making it a secant) or will miss the circle entirely.
If a point P lies outside a circle, there are exactly two tangents to the circle from this point.
Let T₁ and T₂ be the two points where these tangents touch the circle. The segments PT₁ and PT₂ are called the lengths of the tangents from P to the circle.

content.title: Length of a Tangent
content.text: The length of a tangent from an external point to a circle is the distance from the external point to the point of contact on the circle.

topics.topic_name: Theorem 10.2: Equal Lengths of Tangents from External Point

content.title: Theorem Statement
content.text: The lengths of tangents drawn from an external point to a circle are equal.

Proof: Let O be the centre of the circle, P an external point, and PQ and PR be two tangents from P touching the circle at Q and R respectively. We need to prove that PQ = PR. Join OP, OQ, and OR. By Theorem 10.1, ∠OQP = 90° and ∠ORP = 90° (since the tangent is perpendicular to the radius). In right triangles OQP and ORP: OQ = OR (radii of the same circle), OP = OP (common side), and ∠OQP = ∠ORP = 90°. By the RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side) congruence criterion, triangle OQP ≅ triangle ORP. Therefore, PQ = PR (corresponding parts of congruent triangles).
Alternative Proof using Pythagoras Theorem: PQ² = OP² - OQ² and PR² = OP² - OR². Since OQ = OR (radii), we have PQ² = PR², which gives PQ = PR.

content.title: Important Corollary
content.text: The line OP (joining the external point and the centre) bisects the angle ∠QPR. That is, OP is the angle bisector of ∠QPR. This means ∠OPQ = ∠OPR.

topics.topic_name: Worked Example 1: Chord of Concentric Circles

content.text: Prove that in two concentric circles, the chord of the larger circle which touches the smaller circle is bisected at the point of contact.

Solution: Consider two concentric circles C₁ (larger) and C₂ (smaller) with common centre O. Let AB be a chord of the larger circle C₁ that touches the smaller circle C₂ at point P. We need to prove that AP = BP. Join OP. Since AB is tangent to C₂ at P, by Theorem 10.1, OP ⊥ AB. Now, AB is a chord of C₁ and OP is perpendicular to AB. By the property that a perpendicular from the centre of a circle bisects any chord, we conclude that OP bisects AB. Therefore, AP = BP.
Two tangents TP and TQ are drawn to a circle with centre O from an external point T. Prove that ∠PTQ = 2∠OPQ.

Solution: Let ∠PTQ = θ. Since TP = TQ (tangents from external point are equal), triangle TPQ is isosceles. Therefore, ∠TPQ = ∠TQP = (1/2)(180° - θ) = 90° - (θ/2). By Theorem 10.1, ∠OPT = 90° (radius perpendicular to tangent). Thus, ∠OPQ = ∠OPT - ∠TPQ = 90° - (90° - θ/2) = θ/2. This gives ∠PTQ = 2∠OPQ.

topics.topic_name: Worked Example 3: Tangent Length from Chord

content.text: PQ is a chord of length 8 cm of a circle of radius 5 cm. The tangents at P and Q intersect at a point T. Find the length TP.

Solution: Join OT, intersecting PQ at R. Triangle TPQ is isosceles (TP = TQ by Theorem 10.2) and TO is the angle bisector of ∠PTQ. Therefore, OT ⊥ PQ and OT bisects PQ, giving PR = RQ = 4 cm. Using Pythagoras theorem in triangle OPR: OR = √(OP² - PR²) = √(5² - 4²) = √(25 - 16) = 3 cm. Since ∠TPR + ∠RPO = 90° and ∠TPR + ∠PTR = 90°, we have ∠RPO = ∠PTR. Therefore, right triangle TRP is similar to right triangle PRO (by AA similarity). This gives TP/PO = RP/RO, so TP/5 = 4/3, which gives TP = 20/3 cm.
An important property: If two tangents are drawn at the ends of a diameter of a circle, these tangents are parallel to each other.

Proof: Let AB be a diameter with centre O. Let the tangent at A be line ℓ₁ and the tangent at B be line ℓ₂. By Theorem 10.1, ℓ₁ ⊥ OA and ℓ₂ ⊥ OB. Since A, O, and B are collinear (AB is a diameter), both ℓ₁ and ℓ₂ are perpendicular to the same line AB. Therefore, ℓ₁ ∥ ℓ₂ (lines perpendicular to the same line are parallel).
The perpendicular from the centre of a circle to a tangent passes through the point of contact.

This is a direct consequence of Theorem 10.1. If XY is a tangent at point P and O is the centre, then the perpendicular from O to line XY is precisely the line OP, which passes through P (the point of contact).
If an external point is at distance d from the centre and the radius is r, then the length of the tangent from that point is given by: Tangent Length = √(d² - r²) (where d > r)
This formula comes from applying Pythagoras theorem to the right triangle formed by the radius, the tangent, and the line joining the external point to the centre.
When a quadrilateral ABCD is drawn to circumscribe a circle (i.e., all four sides are tangent to the circle), a special property holds: AB + CD = AD + BC.

Proof: Let the circle touch sides AB, BC, CD, and DA at points P, Q, R, and S respectively. By Theorem 10.2, tangents from the same external point are equal. From vertex A: AP = AS. From vertex B: BP = BQ. From vertex C: CQ = CR. From vertex D: DR = DS. Adding the first and third equations: AP + CQ = AS + CR. Adding the second and fourth: BP + DR = BQ + DS. Rearranging: AB + CD = (AP + BP) + (CR + DR) = (AS + BQ) + (CQ + DS) = (AS + DS) + (BQ + CQ) = AD + BC.
When a triangle ABC circumscribes a circle (all three sides are tangent to the circle), tangent segments from each vertex to the two points of tangency on the adjacent sides are equal.

If the circle touches BC at D, CA at E, and AB at F, then: AF = AE (tangents from A), BF = BD (tangents from B), and CD = CE (tangents from C). This property is useful in solving problems involving the perimeter and sides of such triangles.
The angle between two tangents drawn from an external point and the angle subtended by the chord (joining the points of contact) at the centre are supplementary.

Proof: Let P be external, with tangents PT₁ and PT₂ touching the circle at T₁ and T₂. Let ∠PT₁T₂ = θ. Then in quadrilateral OPT₁T₂, we have ∠OT₁P = 90° (radius perpendicular to tangent), ∠OT₂P = 90°, and ∠T₁PT₂ + ∠T₁OT₂ = 360° - 90° - 90° = 180°. Therefore, the angle at P and the angle at O are supplementary.

glossary.definition: A line that intersects a circle at exactly one point, called the point of contact. The tangent is perpendicular to the radius at the point of contact.
A line that intersects a circle at two distinct points. A secant cuts through the circle at these two points.
A line that has no common points with a circle; it neither touches nor intersects the circle.
The single point where a tangent line touches a circle. At this point, the tangent is perpendicular to the radius.
A point located outside the circle, at a distance from the centre greater than the radius.

The distance from an external point to the point of contact where a tangent touches the circle. For an external point at distance d from the centre of a circle with radius r, the tangent length is √(d² - r²).
Two or more circles having the same centre but different radii.
A circle that passes through all vertices of a polygon; the polygon is inscribed in the circle.
A polygon is inscribed in a circle if all its vertices lie on the circle. A polygon circumscribes a circle if all its sides are tangent to the circle.
The line containing the radius through the point of contact of a tangent to a circle. The normal is perpendicular to the tangent.
A line segment joining two points on a circle. A secant contains a chord.
A line segment from the centre of a circle to any point on the circle. All radii of a circle are equal.
The fixed point from which all points on the circle are equidistant. The distance from the centre to any point on the circle is the radius.
A chord that passes through the centre of the circle. The length of the diameter is twice the radius.

summary_points: A circle and a line have three possible relationships: no common points (non-intersecting), two common points (secant), or one common point (tangent).
A tangent to a circle is a line that touches the circle at exactly one point called the point of contact.
The fundamental property: The tangent to a circle at any point is perpendicular to the radius drawn to that point of contact (Theorem 10.1).
At any given point on a circle, there exists exactly one tangent line.
From a point inside a circle, no tangent can be drawn. From a point on the circle, exactly one tangent exists. From a point outside the circle, exactly two tangents can be drawn.
The lengths of two tangents drawn from the same external point to a circle are equal (Theorem 10.2). The line joining the external point and the centre bisects the angle between the two tangents.
For an external point at distance d from the centre of a circle with radius r, the tangent length is √(d² - r²).
Tangents drawn at the ends of a diameter are parallel to each other.
In a quadrilateral circumscribing a circle, the sum of opposite sides are equal: AB + CD = AD + BC.
In a triangle circumscribing a circle, tangent segments from each vertex to the two points of tangency on adjacent sides are equal.
The angle between two tangents from an external point and the angle subtended by the chord (joining points of contact) at the centre are supplementary angles that sum to 180°.
In two concentric circles, any chord of the larger circle that touches the smaller circle is bisected at the point of contact.
Real-world applications of tangent properties include wheel mechanics, pulley systems, and engineering designs where smooth contact and perpendicularity are important.
